content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 30, 2004

The Honorable Steve Messina
City of Benicia
250 East L Street
Benicia, CA 94510

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-200

Dear Mr. Messina:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Does the employment of an attorney by a corporation in which you have a substantial financial interest create a conflict of interest between you and the attorney's other clients?

CONCLUSION

No. Since the attorney is not a source of income to you, the attorney's other clients are not sources of income to you by virtue of this relationship alone.

FACTS

You are the directly elected mayor of the City of Benicia and you have a substantial financial interest in 221 First Street, LLC. 221 First Street, LLC intends to construct a nine-unit commercial/residential project in Benicia and has engaged the services of Jane Dean, Esq. to represent the limited liability company in a development application with the City of Benicia. Ms. Dean has several clients who have applications and other matters pending before the city.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-200
Page No. 2

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests.
(§ 81001, subd. (b).) Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from
"making," "participating in making," or otherwise using his or her official position to
"influence" a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. The
Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for deciding whether an official
has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Reg. 18700, subd. (b)(1) - (8).) Because your
question implicates only one particular step, step three, we proceed directly in the
analysis to that step.²

Step Three – Sources of Income

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from economic interests. These economic interests are described at regulations 18703.1
through 18703.5, inclusive.

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (§ 87103(a); reg. 18703.1(a)); or in
which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position
of management (§ 87103(d); reg. 18703.1(b));

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (§ 87103(b); reg. 18703.2);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (§ 87103(c); reg. 18703.3);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (§ 87103(e);
reg. 18703.4);

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including
those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule.
(§ 87103; reg. 18703.5.)

Your question asks whether you will have a conflict of interest with Ms. Dean's
other clients solely by virtue of your company's hiring of Ms. Dean. Applying the terms
of section 87103 and regulations 18703.1 – 18703.5, however, we see that Ms. Dean,
though your attorney, is not a source of income to you.

_______________
² Steps one and two, whether you are a public official and whether you are participating in
governmental decisions, are presumed met in the circumstances you describe.

File No. A-04-200
Page No. 3

Because Ms. Dean is not a source of income to you, impacts on her business caused
by your decisions affecting the clients of her business need not be considered. (See e.g.,
regulation B 1705.1) In addition, her clients are not, by virtue of this relationship alone,
sources of income to you. As a result, you do not have a conflict of interest with respect
to other clients of Ms. Dean should they have matters before the city in the future. Of
course, if Ms. Dean's clients are independently economic interests of yours as described
above in Section 87103, you may have a conflict of interest when Ms. Dean appears before
you on their behalf. You have provided no facts for us to analyze this further.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchen
General Counsel

By: C. Scott Toche
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
